topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The field of large language models has witnessed significant advancements in recent years, with a focus on improving the Transformer architecture (Gao et al., 2023). One of the key areas of research has been the development of more efficient and scalable models, which has been complemented by the use of retrieval-augmented generation, shown to improve the performance of large language models (Gao et al., 2023). 

In addition to these architectural improvements, there has been a growing interest in applying large language models to specific domains, such as chemistry (Bran et al., 2024) and medicine (Dave Van Veen et al., 2024; Hanwen Xu et al., 2024). For example, adapted large language models have been shown to outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization (Dave Van Veen et al., 2024). Furthermore, whole-slide foundation models have been developed for digital pathology, demonstrating the potential of large language models in real-world medical applications (Hanwen Xu et al., 2024).

The use of large language models in autonomous agents has also been explored, with a survey highlighting the potential benefits and challenges of this approach (Wang et al., 2024). Moreover, the importance of explainability in large language models has been emphasized, with a survey providing an overview of the current state of research in this area (Zhao et al., 2024). In contrast, some studies have focused on the application of large language models in specific tasks, such as medical image segmentation (Risheng Wang et al., 2022) and drug discovery (Zhang et al., 2023).

Despite the significant progress made in large language models, there are still several research gaps that need to be addressed. For example, the development of more comprehensive foundation models that can handle a wide range of tasks and domains is an area that requires further research (Hollmann et al., 2025). Additionally, the integration of large language models with other AI technologies, such as recurrent neural networks (Ibomoiye Domor Mienye et al., 2024), is an area that holds great promise. Overall, the field of large language models is rapidly evolving, with new architectures, applications, and techniques being developed continuously (Naveed et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2024; Gao et al., 2023).

References:
Bran, A. M., Cox, S., & Schilter, O. (2024). Augmenting large language models with chemistry tools. https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-024-00832-8
Dave Van Veen, D. V., Cara Van Uden, C. V., & Louis Blankemeier, L. B. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
Hanwen Xu, H. X., Naoto Usuyama, N. U., & Jaspreet Bagga, J. B. (2024). A whole-slide foundation model for digital pathology from real-world data. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07441-w
Hollmann, N., Müller, S., & Purucker, L. (2025). Accurate predictions on small data with a tabular foundation model. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-08328-6
Ibomoiye Domor Mienye, I. D. M., Theo G. Swart, T. G. S., & George Obaido, G. O. (2024). Recurrent Neural Networks: A Comprehensive Review of Architectures, Variants, and Applications. https://doi.org/10.3390/info15090517
Naveed, H., Khan, A. U., & Qiu, S. (2023). A Comprehensive Overview of Large Language Models. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2307.06435

Zhang, Y., Liu, C., & Liu, M. (2023). Attention is all you need: utilizing attention in AI-enabled drug discovery. https://doi.org/10.1093/bib/bbad467
Zhao, H., Chen, H., & Yang, F. (2024). Explainability for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.1145/3639372
